Summary: In the US, website owners rely on three legal claims against scrapers — copyright infringement, CFAA violations, and trespass to chattels — but case law is still evolving. Key cases include eBay v. Bidder's Edge (trespass), Craigslist v. 3Taps (CFAA), and hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn (Ninth Circuit ruled scraping public data does not violate CFAA). In the EU, jurisdictions vary: Denmark allows systematic crawling of public sites, Ireland enforced a clickwrap ToS against a scraper, and France's CNIL clarified that publicly available data is still personal data protected under GDPR. Other jurisdictions (Australia, India) have narrower or less-settled rules: Australia's Spam Act only covers email harvesting, while India treats ToS violations as contract breaches enforceable under its IT Act."
